content.text: Standard quadrature formulas assume a finite interval and a bounded integrand. **Improper integrals**—those with infinite limits or unbounded integrands—require special treatment. This section develops techniques for handling these challenging cases.

content.title: Classification of Improper Integrals
content.content:
An integral is **improper** if:

1. **Type I (Infinite limits):** $\int_a^{\infty} f(x)\,dx$, $\int_{-\infty}^{b} f(x)\,dx$, or $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx$

2. **Type II (Unbounded integrand):** $\lim_{x \to c} |f(x)| = \infty$ for some $c \in [a, b]$

3. **Type III (Both):** Combinations of infinite limits and singularities

content.title: Types of Improper Integrals
content.problem:
$$
\int_1^{\infty} \frac{1}{x^2}\,dx &= 1 && \text{Type I (infinite upper limit)} \\
\int_0^1 \frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\,dx &= 2 && \text{Type II (singularity at 0)} \\
\int_0^{\infty} e^{-x^2}\,dx &= \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} && \text{Type I} \\
\int_0^1 \frac{\ln x}{\sqrt{x}}\,dx &= -4 && \text{Type II (singularity at 0)}
$$

content.text: The simplest approach replaces infinite limits with large finite values.

content.title: Truncation
content.content:
For $\int_a^{\infty} f(x)\,dx$, choose $B$ large and approximate:
\[
\int_a^{\infty} f(x)\,dx \approx \int_a^{B} f(x)\,dx
\]
The truncation error is $\int_B^{\infty} f(x)\,dx$.

content.title: Truncation Error Bound
content.content:
If $|f(x)| \leq g(x)$ for $x \geq B$ and $\int_B^{\infty} g(x)\,dx$ is known, then
\[
\left|\int_B^{\infty} f(x)\,dx\right| \leq \int_B^{\infty} g(x)\,dx
\]
Common bounds:

- $|f(x)| \leq Cx^{-p}$ with $p > 1$: Error $\leq \frac{C}{(p-1)B^{p-1}}$

- $|f(x)| \leq Ce^{-\alpha x}$ with $\alpha > 0$: Error $\leq \frac{C}{\alpha}e^{-\alpha B}$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Truncation for Exponential Decay
content.solution:
For $x \geq B$, we have $e^{-x^2} \leq e^{-B \cdot x}$ (for $x \geq B \geq 1$), so:
\[
\int_B^{\infty} e^{-x^2}\,dx \leq \int_B^{\infty} e^{-Bx}\,dx = \frac{e^{-B^2}}{B}
\]

For error $< 10^{-10}$: $\frac{e^{-B^2}}{B} < 10^{-10}$ gives $B \approx 5$.

So $\int_0^{\infty} e^{-x^2}\,dx \approx \int_0^5 e^{-x^2}\,dx$ with truncation error $< 10^{-10}$.

Apply composite Simpson's or adaptive quadrature to $\int_0^5 e^{-x^2}\,dx$.

content.text: Transformations can map infinite intervals to finite ones.

content.title: Transformation to Finite Interval
content.solution:
Substitute $x = 1/t$, so $dx = -dt/t^2$ and limits become $t: 1 \to 0$:
\[
\int_1^{\infty} \frac{1}{x^3}\,dx = \int_1^0 t^3 \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{t^2}\right)dt = \int_0^1 t\,dt = \frac{1}{2}
\]

For numerical computation, apply any standard quadrature to $\int_0^1 t\,dt$.

content.title: Transformation for Algebraic Decay
content.content:
For $\int_1^{\infty} f(x)\,dx$ where $f(x) \sim x^{-p}$ as $x \to \infty$ with $p > 1$:

- The substitution $x = 1/t$ gives $\int_0^1 \frac{1}{t^2}f(1/t)\,dt$

- The new integrand behaves like $t^{p-2}$ near $t = 0$

- If $p > 2$, the new integrand is smooth at $t = 0$

- If $1 < p \leq 2$, there is a singularity at $t = 0$ (but integrable)
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Gauss-Laguerre Quadrature
content.content:
For integrals of the form $\int_0^{\infty} e^{-x} g(x)\,dx$:
\[
\int_0^{\infty} e^{-x} g(x)\,dx \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i g(x_i)
\]
where $x_i$ are roots of the Laguerre polynomial $L_n(x)$ and the weights are
\[
w_i = \frac{x_i}{(n+1)^2 [L_{n+1}(x_i)]^2}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text: Gaussian Quadrature for the Full Line

content.title: Gauss-Hermite Quadrature
content.content:
For integrals of the form $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x^2} g(x)\,dx$:
\[
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x^2} g(x)\,dx \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i g(x_i)
\]
where $x_i$ are roots of the Hermite polynomial $H_n(x)$ and the weights are
\[
w_i = \frac{2^{n-1} n! \sqrt{\pi}}{n^2 [H_{n-1}(x_i)]^2}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Singularity Subtraction
content.content:
If $f(x)$ has a known singularity $s(x)$ near $x = a$, write
\[
\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = \int_a^b [f(x) - s(x)]\,dx + \int_a^b s(x)\,dx
\]
If $\int_a^b s(x)\,dx$ is known analytically and $f(x) - s(x)$ is smooth, standard quadrature works well.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Singularity Subtraction
content.solution:
Near $x = 0$: $\sin x \approx x$, so $\frac{\sin x}{\sqrt{x}} \approx \sqrt{x}$.

Write:
\[
\int_0^1 \frac{\sin x}{\sqrt{x}}\,dx = \int_0^1 \left(\frac{\sin x}{\sqrt{x}} - \sqrt{x}\right)dx + \int_0^1 \sqrt{x}\,dx
\]

The second integral is $\frac{2}{3}$. The first integral has integrand:
\[
\frac{\sin x - x}{\sqrt{x}} = \frac{x - x^3/6 + O(x^5) - x}{\sqrt{x}} = -\frac{x^{5/2}}{6} + O(x^{7/2})
\]
which is smooth at $x = 0$. Apply standard quadrature to this integral.

content.title: IMT (Iri-Moriguti-Takahashi) Transformation
content.content:
For $\int_0^1 f(x)\,dx$ where $f$ has an algebraic singularity at $x = 0$, the substitution
\[
x = \psi(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \tanh\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\sinh t\right)\right)
\]
transforms to $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\psi(t))\psi'(t)\,dt$ where $\psi'(t)$ decays double-exponentially at $\pm\infty$, making truncation extremely efficient.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Tanh-Sinh Quadrature
content.content:
The **tanh-sinh** (or double-exponential) transformation is
\[
x = \tanh\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\sinh t\right)
\]
which maps $t \in (-\infty, \infty)$ to $x \in (-1, 1)$ with
\[
\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{\frac{\pi}{2}\cosh t}{\cosh^2\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\sinh t\right)}
\]
The derivative decays like $\exp(-c \cdot e^{|t|})$ (double exponentially) as $|t| \to \infty$.

content.title: Tanh-Sinh for Endpoint Singularities
content.content:
For $\int_{-1}^{1} f(x)\,dx$ where $f$ has integrable singularities at $x = \pm 1$:
$$

\int_{-1}^{1} f(x)\,dx = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\tanh(\frac{\pi}{2}\sinh t)) \cdot \frac{\frac{\pi}{2}\cosh t}{\cosh^2(\frac{\pi}{2}\sinh t)}\,dt
$$
The transformed integrand decays double-exponentially, so the trapezoidal rule converges exponentially fast even with singularities.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Tanh-Sinh Quadrature
content.solution:
Transform $[0, 1]$ to $[-1, 1]$: let $u = 2x - 1$.

The integral becomes $\int_{-1}^{1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-u^2}}\,du = \pi$.

Apply tanh-sinh transformation. With step size $h = 0.1$ and truncating at $|t| \leq 4$:
\[
I \approx h \sum_{k=-40}^{40} w(t_k) f(x(t_k))
\]
where $t_k = kh$, $x(t) = \tanh(\frac{\pi}{2}\sinh t)$, and $w(t)$ is the transformed weight.

With only 81 function evaluations, the result is accurate to 15 digits.
